speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 4, 1996. I'm interviewing Michael Kraus. My name is Jill Greenberg. We're in Brooklyn, Massachusetts, in the United States. This interview is in English. And this is tape number eight.

speaker: interviewee
text: Going to a-- an American university was a real challenge. In many respects. One was to adapt to a life with a family, a growing family. We had three small children and absolutely no understanding of what my-- what I had gone through. My wife's wife-- my cousin's wife is from North Carolina, [INAUDIBLE], the deep South, and she certainly tried very hard, not only to bring up three small children, but also, have this 21-year-old semi-adult that was rather disoriented in the house, and helping him to find his footing in the United States. And looking back, it was probably extremely difficult, challenging task for all of us. I was very absorbed in my studies, and that really took up almost all of my energy. And I think that was fortunate. They tried to shield me completely from the past, from being in touch with, directly or indirectly, with the past, and protected-- what they thought was the right thing to do is never talk about it and bring me into the mainstream of American life, both socially and culturally. And looking back, maybe that was the right thing to do. But obviously, a lot of things got suppressed and only came up much later on. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was eventually thrust into living on my own in New York City when I got my first job after I finished my studies. And that is when I had a feeling that I've really not experienced European culture other than this little town in the middle of Europe as a boy. And I got the yearning to go and discover all the things that I'd been learning in architecture books and felt it hard that I was still a European. And hence, I found myself going back on the boat and for the next six moths, touring Europe, ironically, in a little Volkswagen Beetle that I picked up at the factory in Wolfsburg. It was then the most-- the cheapest and best that you can buy for a limited amount of money. Eventually, I accepted the job in England and then in Geneva, which was the most lucky stroke that I made again because it allowed me to meet Ilana. And if I had to choose the one most fortuitous decision that I had made in my life, it was to accept her hand in marriage, Because she is the most understanding person and enabled me to-- to live with the past while looking ahead and having a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet Ilana?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met Ilana shortly after I arrived in Geneva through a mutual friend, who comes from the same town and who dating an Israeli student who knew Ilana. And we went for a excursion, beautiful Alps and near a lake, and they brought Ilana and me along because they thought we would be a good match. And knowing that, we didn't like each other. And it was, I think, only three years later that by chance we met at an Israeli Independence Celebration at the Maison [? Juif, ?] at the Jewish Center in Geneva. And I immediately recognized her and thought differently about her. And eventually, we had a date and started going out together. She was then in her throes of her final medical exams and was very busy. And I tried to cook for her and be generally supportive. And eventually, we decided that maybe we should go back to America rather than to live in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, even though I liked Israel, I just couldn't image living in that climate and adapting to the way of live there. Uh-huh. On the other hand, I did not want to return to the United State without having somebody like her along. So it worked out very well because she could also get good-- good internship and residency in the States, training that would be good for her anyway. And we then moved to Brooklyn, New York, and had our first child. And when she finished her medical training, we said, well, we can't live in New York. That's not really a livable place. And happened to be in Boston one weekend and decided, well, maybe this is the place to be. And so, we moved here. And since then, it's been a great pleasure to bring up children. And because Ilana was so busy, I was fortunate to spend a great deal of time and effort helping her in their-- in bringing them up. But the unfortunate part of this was that she had a very serious accident shortly after moving. We moved to Boston and she was hospitalized for many, many months. So I really took 100% charge of the children. Tamara was then 6 weeks old. So this was somewhat of a compensation, this concern for my children, that I've lost a family, that I didn't have any family. And so I probably exaggerate the attachment and also, became perhaps even demanding that the children reciprocate in the feeling for-- for the family and expected them to come home and live at home as much as possible. And driving my oldest to Brown and then coming back empty-handed was a real shock for me, my life, that I've lost something. And the-- similarly, her wedding on one hand was the most wonderful feeling, on the other hand, was, again, a feeling that I've lost something.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you talk with your children or tell your children about your experiences in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never really talked about it in any details, not they ask any-- ever any questions. I think that Ilana provided a filter mostly. I didn't keep it from them. But it was, to the best of my memory, seldom a subject we talked about. It was only about 8 or 10 years ago that a-- one of boys, one of the survivors who came from Holland and made a tour of the Untied States and sought out those who were then known to belong to the group. It was a complete surprise to me that these still existed. I only knew about two or three who lived in Israel. But I didn't really attribute any great importance to it. But once he uncovered the names, I became very interested. And what was almost an obsession is that I kept finding out that many events that seemed vivid in my memory in few years after the war have completely vanished or faded. There would be hints of very detailed descriptions in my diary or traces of descriptions that I remembered, the countryside, and the weather, and the sunset, and the people, and the smells, and who knows what. But they have all completely gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was, by finding this group of people, I was hoping that I could connect with them and bring back not only those memories, but speak with them freely about our adventures. I could never talk freely with anybody who has not experienced either identical or similar events. I have no-- in fact, I never talk about it with anybody at my workplace, even though many of them suspect or found out what my background is. But I have no-- understandably no rapport to it. I don't blame them for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, with those who have gone through it, I almost have a desire to talk about it. It doesn't always turn out so well. I was recently in Mexico where one of the boys, one of the survivors lives whom I haven't seen for almost 50 years. Whether by-- by the purposely or by accident, we really never had an opportunity to talk. And somehow, I suspect that mutually, we didn't want to. Either one of our wives was there, and his wife isn't event Jewish, just didn't click. And this happens quite often. But I'm still hoping for the opportunity that it will click. We worked together twice, once in Israel in 1970 and once in Prague 1970. I'm sorry. The date is wrong. In 1990 we were reunited in Israel, and there were about 15 of us. In 1994 we met in Prague, and there were at least 20 or 25 of us. And there, we went into a little retreat, into a little hotel in the countryside for three days And we did talk and reminisce although our spouses were there. That made it a little difficult. So So

speaker: interviewer
text: So where am I today? I have many [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] apprehensions and fears, reactions that stem from events, the experiences of 50 years ago. Five, six years ago, I was very, very depressed. And I sought psychiatric help. And the person that has helped me to overcome a lot of these fears, whom I'm still seeing occasionally, squarely places all the blame to the extreme [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] conditions and experiences that stem from the war. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I have to defer to his professional opinion.

speaker: interviewee
text: The manifestation of those apprehensions and fears occur daily. Small things like taking a shower can evoke the gas chamber, as well as the comfort that I only had cold showers. But the fact that I notice this or make the connection every day, obviously, is something that sticks with me and cannot be erased. A good meal or the fact that I can have another slice of bread is a wonderful feeling, just as it is to crawl into a warm bed and have warm socks. So those are the positive kind of good feelings.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there are many anxieties, a crowd of people on an-- at an airport or milling around a bus station scares me. I dislike waiting in line, evokes some fear of getting somewhere to get something stamped. Or sometimes, I have a very, very difficult time making decisions on the most stupid small things, and, Ilana has to chastise me and see how idiotic it is. But, you know, it comes back. This may be a life and death decision. Do I make a step to the left, step to the right? Do I pack two toothbrushes or one soap? Well, these are the sort of little daily events that color my life. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The fear of retirement that-- or losing my job a few years ago probably motivated that work kept me from being-- having idle thoughts of-- of letting my mind bring me back or bringing on thoughts of death. I'm sure every survivor experience periods when he wanted to-- he or she wanted to commit suicide. And I was not spared those feelings. I think I have now overcome those.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think stopped you from acting out on those impulses?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just don't like messy situations.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, there were long periods where suicide was something that I lived with. Why I didn't do it? I just didn't ever, probably stronger feelings to live, to die. But in the last few years, I think such thoughts were idiotic, to say the least, because I have a lot to be grateful for. And-- and I think I am appreciated, even though I don't always show it. But I hope that when I have more time, that I can go back and face the past and assemble some of the material, as I am doing now, in a more organized fashion. And the-- this threat of the 89 boys intrigues me very much. And I would like to do something and perhaps co-author a real book that's well-written with some professional person, rather than this collection of loosely put-together biographies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why am I doing this? I do believe, even though that this may sound trite, that it is our duty to preserve the memory of so many millions of people who died a horrible death, of good friends and family that will be forgotten when our generation disappears. Whatever the media is to preserve the memory is almost immaterial. But it is my duty to participate in that process and especially since I am probably one of the youngest who is still alive and can help by participating, maybe, in educational programs, as well as word of mouth and written word to preserve the memory. I have one last question before-- before we end. I know you don't have grandchildren yet, but there may be some. What would you want your grandchildren to know about this experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I have somewhat of a problem with my own children because I want to give them everything that maybe I didn't have or what-- or have then fulfill the dreams that I did not realize. And that may not be abnormal for a Jewish mother, which I am often called. But in my case, I-- I am afraid if I stress this too much, that I will give them a guilt feeling. And so-- so I have a diff-- I don't really know the answer what I would like to leave for my family as a personal memoir or a personal remembrance. I think that I am spoking-- speaking more to the future generations in general, rather than in particular of my family. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I thought that this interview would not end properly without my wife, Ilana, being introduced and perhaps saying a few words about how our children were influenced by my experience since you asked the question a little while ago, whether we discussed my war-time wanderings with them at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I recall, I-- we did not talk about your experiences very often until Dana, our oldest, was about 10 years old and learned something about the Holocaust in school. And then there was the movie, shown on television. She was either 10 or 12. And I think she watched it with us. And that was really the first time she understood or realized what you went through in World War II. She was then quite interested and even wrote her term paper when she graduated from school on the Holocaust. I don't think it was a subject we discussed very often, but I think the children realized, you know, that to-- you had a very tough childhood. And some of your peculiar ways of reacting to certain things raising them were related to it, or at least I tried to explain to them that it were related, like eating all your food that's on the plate. They could never understand it. And I think they understood it when they related it to your past experience. But it affected them, I'm sure. But I think we need to talk to them to really figure out in which way it affected them.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father, taken in his favorite coffee house where he went after work to pay-- to play chess.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Karel Kraus. He was born on May 8, 1891 in Nachod.

speaker: interviewer
text: He died--

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in the gas chambers on the 11th of July, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz. My mother, Lotte Kraus nee Goldschmid, was also born in Nachod from the 26 of March, 1898. And she perished in the concentration camp of Stutthof in Poland on the Baltic Sea in January 8, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, start by saying, on the left is my uncle so and so.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is my uncle Hanush Goldschmid. And on the right is my mother, Lotte Goldschmid. This picture was probably taken at the turn of the century, and my uncle is the only survivor of a family of six children. My mother and all her sister perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your uncle-- where did you uncle--

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle died in Rio de Janeiro in 1966.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, stand by. I have a question.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the birth certificate that was issued by the Jewish community and shows that I was born on the 20th of June, 1930, and also gives the date of my circumcision.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself. I'm Michael Kraus. I was nicknamed Misha. And this was probably taken when I was seven to eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that on your Honeymoon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] It's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the title page of the second chapter of my diary, describing arrival in Birkenau-Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was drawn in the convalescent home of [INAUDIBLE] and depicts the entrance to the Ghetto Terezin when I was still doing things from memory shortly after returning to my country. This was taken in 1948 in Prague. It is myself at the age of about 18, walking with my guardian, Rudolf Beck.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] come here. Was it in Prague? Was it in Prague, did he say?
